Critical Reader

Read A Paper PDF

Deep-read one uploaded PDF, ask a focused question, and surface threats, external-validity limits, design weaknesses, and concrete improvements with grounded evidence.
Argument Studio

Evidence Board

Write a claim, pull supporting and limiting papers from your repository, and turn the project into a living argument map instead of a flat paper list.
New Claim
Add a thesis claim, chapter proposition, or research question before running evidence analysis.
Claims

Counter-Evidence Lab

Challenge Stardusts

Store up to five challenge-driven side networks. Each Stardust keeps its own seed paper, sub-target thesis, and imported paper queue outside the main StarMap until you decide what to pull in.
Project Watch

Project Literature Watch

Turn your thesis, abstract, and Core papers into a recurring discovery surface for recent literature. Review recommendations first, then decide what enters StarMap.

Results

Edit Project

Project Settings

Control how search and visualization behave in this project.
Enable Background Precompute
Preloads citation clusters when you open the project so the Citation Cluster modal can start faster later. Semantic theme clusters are computed only when you open them manually. Recommended ON for higher-performance devices, OFF when conserving battery.
Use LLM Theme Naming
Uses your configured LLM API to rename Theme Cluster labels into clearer wording. This only updates labels and does not change clustering assignments.
Preload Zotero Sync
Background-load Zotero folders and unmatched-paper previews when you open this project, so the Zotero Sync panel opens instantly later.
Use citation-count quality assist
When enabled, similarity blends text relevance with a compressed citation-count score from OpenAlex. This only adjusts ranking similarity, not the core text embeddings.
Show Rainbow Lines for Top 10 Target Links
In Network mode, color the 10 strongest links from the target thesis with an internal rainbow gradient. Default OFF to keep the bi-directional StarMap visually cleaner.
Keep Trail Arrows Visible When Trails Is Minimized
When the Trails floating panel is collapsed, keep the currently active trail overlay visible on the StarMap chart. Turn this OFF if minimizing Trails should also hide its arrows.
Citation Weight Strength
Citation count is compressed with min(log(1 + citations) / log(1 + 200), 1) before blending into similarity.
Similarity Scores Need Refresh
Keywords, abstract, or current content changed. Re-run similarity scoring to refresh the project ranking.
Danger Zone
To avoid accidental clicks in the workspace, clearing all related papers now lives here. Right-click only. Left-click does nothing.

StarMap System Introduction

Start here if you are new to StarMap. This guide explains what the system is for, what each workspace does, and the smoothest way to get useful results without getting lost.
System Overview
Use StarMap as a Thesis-Guided Research Operating System
StarMap works best when one project represents one real research direction: a thesis, chapter argument, dissertation section, or tightly framed question. You build a focused repository, inspect how papers relate semantically and by citation, test claims against evidence and counter-evidence, deep-read the most important PDFs, and keep discovery running through watch workflows. The point is not to accumulate the largest library. The point is to keep the most decision-relevant papers visible, explainable, and ready to support writing.
1 project = 1 focused research question
Map, read, argue, and monitor in one workspace
Main repository and counter-evidence lab stay separate
1
Set Up One Clear Project
Begin with one target title, one abstract, and any draft writing you already have. StarMap uses that framing text to rank papers, interpret clusters, guide watches, and explain relevance, so better project framing improves almost every later result.
2
Import a Focused Seed Library
Use local PDFs or Zotero to bring in a small but strong starting set. StarMap performs better with a clean seed library than with a huge mixed dump, because these first papers become the basis for similarity ranking, citation structure, and later theme discovery.
3
Curate Before You Expand
Treat Top Related Papers and All Papers as your main curation surfaces. Review titles, abstracts, notes, and statuses such as Core, Pending, Unread, and Underweight before you start importing broadly.
4
Learn the Structure of the Repository
Workspace Atlas lets you view the same repository in Orbital, Network, and Citation Graph form. Use it to spot clusters, bridge papers, citation neighborhoods, and gaps in coverage before you make decisions about what to read or import next.
5
Use the Analysis Workspaces Intentionally
Read A Paper PDF is for deep critique of one paper at a time, using its real uploaded PDF. Evidence Board is for testing a claim against supporting and limiting evidence. Challenge Stardusts are for building separate counter-evidence side networks without polluting the main repository too early.
6
Then Scale Out With Clusters and Watch
Once the repository is reasonably clean, use Semantic Cluster to group papers by topic, Citation Cluster to detect citation communities, and Project Literature Watch to monitor recent target papers, scholars, and journals without leaving the project workflow.
What Each Major Workspace Is For
StarMap Visualization helps you understand structure. All Papers is the project-wide search and filtering hub. Read A Paper PDF helps you interrogate one important paper in depth rather than skimming blindly.
Argument and Counter-Argument
Evidence Board is where a claim meets supporting and limiting evidence from your repository. Challenge Stardusts let you isolate rebuttal trails and import only the counter-evidence that survives inspection.
Maintenance and Growth
Repository Analytics shows whether your library is clean enough for good ranking and clustering. Zotero Sync, Cited By, References, and Literature Watch help you expand only after the current repository is under control.
Recommended First-Pass Workflow
If you are using StarMap for the first time, a reliable sequence is: set up the project clearly → import a focused seed set → clean and label papers in Top Related Papers and All Papers → inspect the structure in StarMap Visualization → deep-read a few key papers → create claims in Evidence Board → build Challenge Stardusts only when you need structured counter-evidence → run clustering when the repository is stable → use Literature Watch to keep the frontier current.
The practical rule is simple: curate first, expand second. StarMap becomes much more useful when the main repository stays tight, your claims are explicit, and counter-evidence is tested in its own workspace before you merge it into the project.
